---
title: "FW 536 — Day 3 morning lab (v3 · aquatic & fisheries) — Your Name"
author: "Your Name Here"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    self_contained: true
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: show
    theme: cosmo
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```

# Day 3 morning lab — Hierarchical modeling problem set

**Problem set version:** **v3 — aquatic & fisheries theme**.
(The companion version is [v2 — terrestrial wildlife](problem_set_v2.html); you choose whichever theme fits your interests.)

This template matches [`problem_set_v3.html`](problem_set_v3.html). Read each problem statement there, write your work below, **Knit** to HTML, and upload the `.html` to the matching Canvas assignment.

For setup help see the [R Markdown tutorial](../rmarkdown_tutorial.html).

---

# Setup

```{r}
# Load packages you'll need. Add or remove as appropriate.
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(lme4)
library(nlme)
library(AICcmodavg)
library(MuMIn)
```

---

# Problem 1 — Chinook salmon redds nested in streams

> A redd survey covers 15 streams in the Willamette basin; each stream is walked once and the number of Chinook redds per 100 m of stream is recorded for 4 segments per stream. The science question: are mean redd densities meaningfully different among streams, and can we use the...

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 2 — Stream temperature loggers across watersheds and years

> You deploy temperature loggers in 6 watersheds, 5 sites per watershed, sampled in 3 years. The response is summer mean temperature. You want to test whether shading (canopy cover %) predicts temperature, after accounting for watershed and year.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 3 — Steelhead growth: individual-level $L_\infty$

> You have repeated length-at-age data for 25 PIT-tagged steelhead, each measured 4–10 times over 5 years. Fit a fixed-effect von Bertalanffy via nls and a mixed-effect version via nlme with a random $L_\infty$ per fish.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 4 — Sockeye smolt detection at PIT arrays

> 20,000 PIT-tagged sockeye smolts pass through 8 mainstem detection arrays. Each tag is observed at each array (1) or not (0). You fit:

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 5 — Pacific lamprey emergence × temperature, across rivers

> You have 8 rivers and 5 years of lamprey emergence counts vs spring water temperature. You fit:

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# (Optional) Embed a photo of hand-written work

For problems easier to do on paper, photograph and embed:

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE, out.width="60%", fig.cap="Hand-derivation for Problem N"}
knitr::include_graphics("images/problemN_derivation.jpg")
```

(Change `eval=FALSE` to `eval=TRUE` after saving an image. See the [R Markdown tutorial](../rmarkdown_tutorial.html) for details.)

---

# Submission checklist

- [ ] Answered every morning problem from `problem_set_v3.html`.
- [ ] Knitted to HTML without errors.
- [ ] Photos (if any) embedded with `self_contained: true` in the YAML.
- [ ] Uploaded the `.html` to the matching Canvas assignment.
